volumeTitle: Persians. Seven against Thebes. Suppliants. Prometheus Bound
label: Persians (Section 232 - Section 385)

sections.label: Section 232
sections.original: τῆλε πρὸς δυσμαῖς ἄνακτος Ἡλίου φθινασμάτων .
sections.translation: Far from here , to the west where the last rays of our Lord the Sun set .

sections.label: Section 233
sections.original: ἀλλὰ μὴν ἵμειρ ἐμὸς παῖς τήνδε θηρᾶσαι πόλιν ;
sections.translation: Can it then really be that my son had the keen desire to make this city his prey ?

sections.label: Section 234
sections.original: πᾶσα γὰρ γένοιτ ἂν Ἑλλὰς βασιλέως ὑπήκοος .
sections.translation: Yes , for then all Hellas would be subject to the King .

sections.label: Section 235
sections.original: ὧδέ τις πάρεστιν αὐτοῖς ἀνδροπλήθεια στρατοῦ ;
sections.translation: Does their army have such a multitude of men ?

sections.label: Section 236
sections.original: καὶ στρατὸς τοιοῦτος , ἔρξας πολλὰ δὴ Μήδους κακά .
sections.translation: Yes , it is an army of such magnitude that it has caused great disaster for the Medes .

sections.label: Section 237
sections.original: καὶ τί πρὸς τούτοισιν ἄλλο ; πλοῦτος ἐξαρκὴς δόμοις ;
sections.translation: And what else have they besides ? Do they have sufficient wealth in their homes ?

sections.label: Section 238
sections.original: ἀργύρου πηγή τις αὐτοῖς ἐστι , θησαυρὸς χθονός .
sections.translation: Of silver they possess a veritable fountain , a treasure chest in their soil .

sections.label: Section 239
sections.original: πότερα γὰρ τοξουλκὸς αἰχμὴ διὰ χερῶν [*] αὐτοῖς [*] πρέπει ;
sections.translation: Is the bow-stretching arrow particularly suited to their hands ?

sections.label: Section 240
sections.original: οὐδαμῶς : ἔγχη σταδαῖα καὶ φεράσπιδες σαγαί .
sections.translation: Far from it ; they have lances for close fight and shields that serve them for armor .

sections.label: Section 241
sections.original: τίς δὲ ποιμάνωρ ἔπεστι κἀπιδεσπόζει στρατῷ ;
sections.translation: And who is set over them as shepherd and is master of their host ?

sections.label: Section 242
sections.original: οὔτινος δοῦλοι κέκληνται φωτὸς οὐδ ὑπήκοοι .
sections.translation: Of no man are they called the slaves or vassals .

sections.label: Section 243
sections.original: πῶς ἂν οὖν μένοιεν ἄνδρας πολεμίους ἐπήλυδας ;
sections.translation: How then can they withstand the attack of an invading foe ?

sections.label: Section 244
sections.original: ὥστε Δαρείου πολύν τε καὶ καλὸν φθεῖραι στρατόν .
sections.translation: So well as to have destroyed Darius ’ great and courageous host .

sections.label: Section 245
sections.original: δεινά τοι λέγεις κιόντων [*] τοῖς τεκοῦσι φροντίσαι .
sections.translation: In truth , your words have given the fathers and mothers of those who are now on their way there dire food for thought .

sections.label: Section 246
sections.original: ἀλλ ἐμοὶ δοκεῖν τάχ εἴσει πάντα ναμερτῆ [*] λόγον .
sections.translation: No , rather I think that you will soon learn the truth of the matter . For here comes one who is beyond a doubt a Persian courier . He bears clear tidings of some issue , be it good or bad .

sections.label: Section 249
sections.original: ὦ γῆς ἁπάσης Ἀσιάδος πολίσματα ,
sections.translation: O cities of all the land of Asia ,

sections.label: Section 250
sections.original: ὦ Περσὶς αἶα καὶ μέγας [*] πλούτου λιμήν ,
sections.translation: O realm of Persia , and bounteous haven of wealth , at a single stroke all your plenteous prosperity has been shattered , and the flower of the Persians has fallen and perished ! Ah , it is a terrible task to be the first to deliver news of disaster . And yet , Persians , I must relate the entirety of the calamity

sections.label: Section 255
sections.original: Πέρσαι : στρατὸς γὰρ πᾶς ὄλωλε βαρβάρων .
sections.translation: — the whole barbarian host is lost .

sections.label: Section 1.256
sections.original: ἄνι ἄνια κακὰ
sections.translation: Grievous , grievous disaster , strange and cruel . Alas , Persians , weep now that you hear of this calamity .

sections.label: Section 1.260
sections.original: ὡς πάντα γ ἔστ ἐκεῖνα διαπεπραγμένα :
sections.translation: Yes , weep , for you hear that the whole army is utterly destroyed ; and I myself unexpectedly behold the day of my return .

sections.label: Section 1.262
sections.original: ἦ μακροβίοτος
sections.translation: The life which has been given to us elders is too long , for we have now heard

sections.label: Section 1.265
sections.original: τόδε πῆμ ἄελπτον .
sections.translation: of this unexpected misery .

sections.label: Section 1.266
sections.original: καὶ μὴν παρών γε [*] κοὐ λόγους ἄλλων κλύων ,
sections.translation: Since I myself was present and did not merely hear what happened from the report of others , I can tell you exactly what kind of disaster was wrought .

sections.label: Section 2.268
sections.original: ὀτοτοτοῖ , [*] μάταν
sections.translation: Alas , alas ! In vain did our vast and variously armed host

sections.label: Section 2.270
sections.original: γᾶσδ [*] ἀπ Ἀσίδος ἦλθεν [*] αἴας
sections.translation: go forth from the land of Asia against the hostile soil of Hellas .

sections.label: Section 2.272
sections.original: πλήθουσι νεκρῶν δυσπότμως ἐφθαρμένων
sections.translation: Full of the bodies of men who perished by a miserable fate are the shores of Salamis and all the neighboring coasts .

sections.label: Section 2.274
sections.original: ὀτοτοτοῖ , φίλων
sections.translation: Alas , alas ! You say that the bodies of our loved ones ,

sections.label: Section 2.275
sections.original: ἁλίδονα μέλεα [*] πολυβαφῆ
sections.translation: battered by the brine and drenched , are tossing , washed back and forth among the reefs .

sections.label: Section 2.278
sections.original: οὐδὲν γὰρ ἤρκει τόξα , πᾶς δ ἀπώλλυτο
sections.translation: Our bows were of no use , and the whole host has perished , overwhelmed when ship charged on ship .

sections.label: Section 3.280
sections.original: ἴυζ ἄποτμον Πέρσαις
sections.translation: Raise a doleful and mournful wail for the Persians , the wretched Persians , since they have met with complete and utter ruin . Alas for the destruction of our host !

sections.label: Section 3.284
sections.original: ὦ πλεῖστον ἔχθος ὄνομα Σαλαμῖνος κλύειν :
sections.translation: O name of Salamis most odious to my ears !

sections.label: Section 3.285
sections.original: φεῦ , τῶν Ἀθηνῶν ὡς στένω μεμνημένος .
sections.translation: Alas , how I groan when I recall the memory of Athens !

sections.label: Section 3.286
sections.original: στυγναί γ Ἀθᾶναι [*] δᾴοις :
sections.translation: Ah , hateful indeed is Athens to her foes . Now must we remember how many Persian women she has deprived of sons and husbands , lost all in vain .

sections.label: Section 290
sections.original: σιγῶ πάλαι δύστηνος ἐκπεπληγμένη
sections.translation: Long have I kept silent in my misery , struck with dismay at our disaster ; for this calamity is so great that it is not possible to say or even to ask about its extent . Nevertheless mortals must endure affliction when it is heaven-sent .

sections.label: Section 295
sections.original: λέξον καταστάς , κεἰ στένεις κακοῖς ὅμως ,
sections.translation: Compose yourself , and even though you groan at our loss , relate the sum of our disaster and speak out ! Who is there that is not dead ? Whom of our leaders must we bewail ? Who , appointed to wield command , by death left his post empty , without its chief ?

sections.label: Section 299
sections.original: Ξέρξης μὲν αὐτὸς ζῇ τε καὶ βλέπει φάος . [*]
sections.translation: Xerxes himself lives and beholds the light .

sections.label: Section 300
sections.original: ἐμοῖς μὲν εἶπας δώμασιν φάος μέγα
sections.translation: The words you utter bring a great light of joy into my house , and bright day after night wrapped in gloom .

sections.label: Section 302
sections.original: Ἀρτεμβάρης δὲ μυρίας ἵππου [*] βραβεὺς
sections.translation: But Artembares , commander of ten thousand cavalrymen , is being battered now against Silenia ’ s cruel shore . And Dadaces , leader of a thousand men , leapt , struck by a spear ,

sections.label: Section 305
sections.original: πήδημα κοῦφον ἐκ νεὼς ἀφήλατο :
sections.translation: with a nimble bound from his ship . Tenagon , the true-born Bactrians ’ chieftain , is ranging now around the surf-beaten isle of Ajax . Lilaeus and Arsames , and , third , Argestes ,

sections.label: Section 310
sections.original: δινούμενοι [*] κύρισσον ἰσχυρὰν χθόνα :
sections.translation: kept buffeting against its rugged shore , whirled around about the island , [*] the breeding-place of doves . Arcteus , too , who lived by the waters of the Egyptian Nile , Adeues , and Pharnuchus of the mighty shield — all these were hurled out of one ship . Matallus of Chrysa , commander of ten thousand ,

sections.label: Section 315
sections.original: ἵππου μελαίνης ἡγεμὼν τρισμυρίας ,
sections.translation: leader of the Black Cavalry , thirty thousand strong , in death dyed red his thick and shaggy beard , changing its color with a deep crimson stain . Arabus , too , the Magian , perished there , and Bactrian Artabes , a settler now in a rugged land .

sections.label: Section 320
sections.original: Ἄμιστρις Ἀμφιστρεύς τε πολύπονον δόρυ
sections.translation: Amistris , and Amphistreus , wielder of a painful spear , and brave Ariomardus , whose death brought grief to Sardis , and Seisames the Mysian , and Tharybis , admiral of five times fifty ships , a Lyrnaean by descent , a man of physical beauty ,

sections.label: Section 325
sections.original: κεῖται θανὼν δείλαιος οὐ μάλ εὐτυχῶς :
sections.translation: lies dead in a state of misery , no longer attended by good fortune . [*] Syennesis , also , the governor of the Cilicians , foremost in courage , he whose prowess did the foe most harm , found there a glorious death . Such were the leaders about whom I was making my report .

sections.label: Section 330
sections.original: πολλῶν παρόντων δ [*] ὀλίγ ἀπαγγέλλω κακά .
sections.translation: Although our losses were many , I announce but few .

sections.label: Section 331
sections.original: αἰαῖ , κακῶν ὕψιστα δὴ κλύω τάδε ,
sections.translation: Alas ! The words I hear put the very crown upon our woes ; a disgrace to the Persians and cause for shrill lament . But retrace your tale and tell me this clearly :

sections.label: Section 335
sections.original: ὥστ ἀξιῶσαι Περσικῷ στρατεύματι
sections.translation: how great was the number of the Greek ships which gave them confidence enough to go into battle with their armed prows against the Persian army ?

sections.label: Section 337
sections.original: πλήθους μὲν ἂν σάφ ἴσθ ἕκατι βάρβαρον [*]
sections.translation: If numbers had been the only factor , be assured that the barbarians would have gained the victory with their fleet . For the whole number of the ships of Hellas amounted to ten times thirty ,

sections.label: Section 340
sections.original: ναῶν , δεκὰς δ ἦν τῶνδε χωρὶς ἔκκριτος :
sections.translation: and , in addition to these , there was a chosen squadron of ten . But Xerxes , this I know , had under his command a thousand , while those excelling in speed were twice a hundred , and seven more . This is the total of their respective numbers . Do you think that we were simply outnumbered in this contest ?

sections.label: Section 345
sections.original: ἀλλ ὧδε δαίμων τις κατέφθειρε στρατόν ,
sections.translation: No , it was some divine power that tipped the scale of fortune with unequal weight and thus destroyed our host . The gods preserve the city of the goddess Pallas .

sections.label: Section 348
sections.original: ἔτ ἆρ Ἀθηνῶν ἔστ ἀπόρθητος πόλις ;
sections.translation: Is then the city of Athens not yet despoiled ?

sections.label: Section 349
sections.original: ἀνδρῶν γὰρ ὄντων ἕρκος ἐστὶν ἀσφαλές .
sections.translation: No , while her men still live , her ramparts are impregnable .

sections.label: Section 350
sections.original: ἀρχὴ δὲ ναυσὶ συμβολῆς τίς ἦν , φράσον :
sections.translation: But the beginning of the encounter of the fleets , tell me about it . Who began the onset ? Was it the Hellenes ? Or my son , exulting in the multitude of his ships ?

sections.label: Section 353
sections.original: ἦρξεν μέν , ὦ δέσποινα , τοῦ παντὸς κακοῦ
sections.translation: My Queen , some destructive power or evil spirit , appearing from somewhere or other , caused the beginning of our utter rout .

sections.label: Section 355
sections.original: ἀνὴρ γὰρ Ἕλλην ἐξ Ἀθηναίων στρατοῦ
sections.translation: A Hellene , from the Athenian host , came to your son Xerxes and told this tale : that , when the gloom of black night should set in , the Hellenes would not remain in place , but , springing upon the rowing benches of their ships , would seek , some here , some there ,

sections.label: Section 360
sections.original: δρασμῷ κρυφαίῳ βίοτον ἐκσωσοίατο .
sections.translation: to preserve their lives by stealthy flight . But Xerxes , when he heard this , comprehending neither the cleverness of the Greek nor that the gods grudged him success , straightway gave all his captains orders to this effect — that , when the sun had ceased to illumine the earth with his beams ,

sections.label: Section 365
sections.original: λήξῃ , κνέφας δὲ τέμενος αἰθέρος λάβῃ ,
sections.translation: and darkness had covered the region of the sky , they should bring up in a tight group the main body of the fleet , disposed in triple line , to bar the exits and the sounding straits , and station other ships in a circle around the island of Ajax . He gave them a warning too that , should the Hellenes escape an evil doom ,

sections.label: Section 370
sections.original: ναυσὶν κρυφαίως δρασμὸν εὑρόντες τινά ,
sections.translation: finding by stealth some means of flight for their fleet , it had been decreed that every captain should lose his head . These commands he made with complete confidence of heart , since he knew not the issue intended by the gods . Our crews then , with no lack of order but with an obedient spirit ,

sections.label: Section 375
sections.original: δεῖπνόν τ [*] ἐπορσύνοντο , ναυβάτης τ ἀνὴρ
sections.translation: prepared their evening meal , while each sailor looped his oar about its thole-pin so that it fitted well . But when the light of the sun had faded and night drew on , each master of an oar and each man versed in arms went on board .

sections.label: Section 380
sections.original: τάξις δὲ τάξιν παρεκάλει νεὼς μακρᾶς :
sections.translation: The long galleys cheered each other , line by line ; and they held their course as each captain had been ordered , and all through the night the commanders of the fleet kept their whole force cruising to and fro across the strait . Night began to wane ,

sections.label: Section 385
sections.original: κρυφαῖον ἔκπλουν οὐδαμῇ καθίστατο :
sections.translation: yet the fleet of the Hellenes in no way attempted to put forth by stealth . When , however , radiant Day with her white horses shone over all the land , a loud cheer like a song of triumph first rang out from the Hellenes , and , at the same instant ,
